title: Barrow students lift voices for Russians, Croatians
summary: The yellow sweatshirts stood out in the speedskating stands. So did the words on them: "Barrow Whalers." The group of eight students and two chaperones from Barrow High School had come as far as some of the Special Olympics athletes, all to cheer like mad for some Russians they'd never met. Barrow students pose with Croatian skier Kristian Skrobonja, with the yellow helmet, after the first run of the event Thursday at Alyeska. title: ADVENTURERS REVEAL THEIR FAVORITE GEAR
summary: With the car loaded, you jump in and go, headed for a thrilling weekend of climbing rock walls, hiking pristine valleys or casting into distant emerald lakes for trout. Then you get there, pull out your gear, start packing for the trek and notice something odd. Something just doesn't feel right. Then reality sets in. You've forgotten your most treasured piece of equipment, that one piece of gear that always -- no matter what -- accompanies every adventure. It's bad karma. That dream weekend may not be looking too bright after all. The Daily News recently asked a variety of Alaska outdoors men and women which piece of equipment they would be lost without. We're talking comfort gear, and we're assuming all outdoor-savvy people have the sense to carry the necessities for their safety. So, map…

title: Face the fact: Fossil fuels run our lives
summary: Finger Lake -- The outhouses at Winter Lake Lodge here disappeared sometime in mid- December. They are invisible now. The entrances are five or six big steps down into snow. Lodge owner Carl Dixon said there was one week when 87 inches of snow fell. In the last two days, another couple of feet have fallen. This is how glaciers form. In Anchorage, the warming -- call it global warming if you buy the prevailing theory -- comes as rain. Here it comes as snow. Climatologists confess these are the kinds of differences that make it difficult to model global weather. Warming might mean the glaciers melt and retreat. It might also mean new glaciers form. Quite possibly it could mean both. Warming could shrink low-elevation coastal glaciers while at the same time growing their higher and more inter…

title: State trolling for answers to drop in fishing-license sales
summary: Fairbanks -- Fewer Alaskans are getting hooked on fishing and the Alaska Department of Fish and Game wants to find out why, because as the number of fishermen shrinks, so does the agency's budget. Fish and Game began mailing out 8,500 surveys last month week to try and learn why sales of resident fishing licenses have dropped by 15,000 since 1993. While Alaska's population grew by 4 percent from 1993 to 1999, resident fishing license sales declined nearly 8 percent, from 191,000 in 1993 to 176,000 in 1999. "Without people interested in maintaining the resource it's very hard to make a case for sustaining the longevity of that resource," said Bill Romberg, a fisheries biologist in Anchorage who spearheaded the survey. "If people are just losing interest in fishing, that's one thing," Romber…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Conservationists sue, call Game Board illicit By Elizabeth Manning Daily News Reporter (Published December 3, 2000) After decades of debate over wolf control, hunting closures, bear baiting, bear viewing -- a panoply of animal issues -- conservationists have gone to court claiming that the current Alaska Board of Game is illegal. They have claimed in Superior Court that the board, which sets wildlife policy, is monopolized by hunters and trappers and that their goal is to give average Alaskans a direct voice in wildlife decisions. The lawsuit, filed Monday, says the board violates the state constitutional principle that wildlife resources belong to all Alaskans. It claims the board makeup violates a state law stipulating that the board should "provide diversity of interest and points o…

The competition teams up a Special Olympics skater with a professional or high-level amateur skater. title: Race among the rookies turns up with surprises
summary: Mcgrath -- Rookie musher Gwen Holdmann has too sunny a personality to be this year's dark horse. But that's precisely the role the cheerful Fairbanks musher was playing on the fourth day of the 29th Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race. Other Iditarod competitors and the media took notice when Holdmann pulled into McGrath at 1:39 a.m. Tuesday. For a while, Holdmann became leading rookie in this year's race. "She's got a hell of a team and she seems happy," said Iditarod veteran Tim Osmar, who trailed Holdmann by several hours. "She's someone we weren't really thinking about. As it is, she's kicking our butt." The musher with long straw-colored hair arrived in McGrath ahead of Thomas Tetz and well ahead of Aliy Zirkle, both considered before the race began to be the rookies to watch. Anchorage rook…

title: Two hotels are headed for Midtown
summary: Two hotels are in the works for one of the last large tracts of undeveloped land in Midtown Anchorage. And a developer hopes the hotels will spark restaurants, offices and perhaps retail to fill in the rest of the property. The first hotel -- a 125-room Hilton Garden Inn -- would be built near the southeast corner of Tudor Road and C Street. Navin Dimond, president of Denver-based Stonebridge Companies, said Wednesday he recently bought the land and plans to begin construction on the four-story hotel this summer, with it opening next year. His firm also owns the nearby Hampton Inn Hotel. Another hotel is planned for the northeast corner of C and International Airport Road, said Bruce Burnett, an Anchorage developer. summary: Gov. Tony Knowles is heading the wrong way with plans for a natural gas pipeline to the Lower 48, the head of a company proposing an alternative project said Thursday. Jeff Lowenfels, president and CEO of Yukon Pacific Corp., sees many flaws with a plan to pipe Alaska North Slope gas along the Alaska Highway through Canada. Yukon Pacific wants to build a gas pipeline along the trans-Alaska oil pipeline route to Valdez and ship it in a liquefied form to markets in Asia and possibly the Lower 48 and Mexico. title: Moose, wolf plan proposed
summary: Though disagreement continues about what caused the moose population to crash in the first place, a governor-appointed task force has recommended a compromise plan that could lead to killing bears and wolves to protect moose in the Interior region around McGrath. It's not predator control yet, but it's the closest that opposing parties in the state's wolf wars have come in years toward accepting a wolf kill program that might also win approval from Gov. Tony Knowles. It also goes one step further and recommends the baiting of black bears to make them easier for hunters to kill or even the possibility of trapping and killing of bears by the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. Bears and wolves prey on moose and caribou. Residents around McGrath and in other parts of the state have long asked…

title: Weather delays ski event
summary: Lots of new snow and strong winds at Mount Alyeska in Girdwood on Monday forced a one-day postponement of the alpine ski event of the 2001 Special Olympics World Winter Games. "They made the decision for the safety of the athletes," said Stacy Schubert, a spokeswoman for the Games. Over a period of about 24 hours ending at 2:30 p.m., the mountain received more than two feet of fresh snow at the top and more than a foot at the bottom, said Alyeska snow safety director Jim Kennedy. Winds at the top were blowing about 20 mph with stronger gusts, creating potential instability on the chairlift and possible avalanche conditions, he said. The 288 athletes from 33 countries participating in the alpine skiing competition are scheduled to race today in two downhill time trials, one from 10 a.m. to …

title: Refuge lobbyists up ante
summary: Conservation groups are staging a nationwide call-in today urging President Clinton to designate the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge a national monument. Just 18 days remain in Clinton's presidency. Although Clinton has said he doesn't think making a monument would afford the area additional protection, conservationists say they will continue lobbying until the final hours of his administration. The stakes are real. President-elect George W. Bush has pledged to open up the Arctic refuge to exploration and drilling and has so far appointed two key Cabinet members -- secretary of interior and secretary of energy -- who appear to favor oil development in ANWR. summary: The Palmer district attorney's office is reviewing the death of a pipeline worker in Valdez last summer to explore possible criminal charges against Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. and its equipment maintenance contractor, Houston/NANA Inc. Jerry Barnes, 51, died Aug. 16 when five of six brakes on his dump truck failed as he descended a steep, winding road. The loaded, 30-ton truck rolled off a 50-foot cliff. Not only did Barnes have little training to drive a dump truck, but the road was supposed to have been closed to heavy vehicles, according to state and federal officials. Investigators also faulted Alyeska and Houston/NANA for inadequate maintenance procedures in Valdez. title: FINAL Alaska Airlines inspection plan aborted by FAA
summary: A few weeks after Alaska Airlines Flight 261 plunged into the Pacific a year ago with 88 people aboard, top Federal Aviation Administration officials planned an unusual "collaborative inspection" of the airline. The plan, which could have allowed Alaska to escape enforcement action and civil penalties, was scrapped after 64 airline mechanics complained they'd been pressured to cut corners on maintenance and repairs. The aborted plan is disclosed in recently released interviews of Alaska and FAA officials conducted late last year by the National Transportation Safety Board. The Seattle Post-Intelligencer reported on the material Wednesday. summary: Voters dealt a crushing blow to the property tax initiative Tuesday, choosing to continue local government spending at current levels rather than pocket the savings from reduced taxes. Click here for latest vote totals With about three-fourths of the votes counted statewide, fewer than three voters in 10 supported the tax cap, which was closely modeled from California's Proposition 13 of 1978. More voters wanted to legalize marijuana this year than supported the tax cap. The landslide defeat was larger than margins in other lopsided votes in recent past, including the 1998 referendums opposing same-sex marriages and favoring English only in government. Juli Jonas, a member of the Girdwood Board Of Supervisors, is not convinced that a railroad spur into Girdwood makes sense for her community right now. title: Insurance grounds Kenai helicopters
summary: A Kenai helicopter company has all but grounded its fleet because of trouble getting enough affordable insurance. The problem for Kenai Air Alaska Inc. isn't a bad safety record, said owner Craig Lofstedt. Rather, it's an intense hardening of the insurance market that is putting the squeeze on Alaska aviation companies. It's a crisis that reflects the state's chronic high rate of accidents and could lead to higher rates and less seat availability for travelers using Alaska's small commercial air carriers, aviation and insurance industry professionals say. Other small airlines and air taxis are scrambling to find affordable coverage amid a steady tenor from fliers, insurers, the Federal Aviation Administration and others that Alaska is paying the price for a high rate of accidents. The Associated Press (Published March 6, 2001) Anchorage -- Operators of six now-closed Alaska Marketplace stores have filed for arbitration against Safeway Inc., the former owner of the stores. Seattle-based Associated Grocers Inc. and Northwest Retail Ventures LLC filed a demand for arbitration last month with the American Arbitration Association, said Robert Hoyt, Northwest Retail Ventures chairman. title: Country moose, city moose
summary: Anchorage moose may seem at ease munching willows in Kincaid Park or strolling suburban streets, but science suggests the city moose live with twice the stress as their rural counterparts. What's more, a study examining the levels of stress hormone in moose poop concluded that moose in Kincaid Park were the most anxiety-ridden of three groups studied in Anchorage. title: 9 EATERIES CITED FOR ALCOHOL SALES
summary: The price of a beer skyrocketed in some parts of Anchorage this week, costing at least one restaurant server a job and putting owners of nine local eateries on notice for selling alcohol to underaged customers. Anchorage police, using 18-year-old volunteers, cited nine of 10 restaurants targeted in a sting operation Tuesday and Wednesday nights. Only the Red Robin restaurant on B Street carded the young patron and refused to sell spirits. At La Mex in Spenard, a pink slip came with the police citation. title: RUSSIAN RIVER BEAR SHOOTING LEGAL AS A HUNT, OFFICIALS SAY
summary: The man who shot a familiar black bear on the Russian River over the weekend, saying he feared it would attack his daughter, will not be prosecuted for the wanton taking of game, authorities said Thursday. State and federal agencies investigated the Sunday afternoon incident, in which 35-year-old Philip Rickard of Anchorage shot a subadult male bear that many people had characterized as a nonaggressive and familiar presence along the river. They have closed the case, having determined that it was a legitimate defense shooting, and if not that, it would have been a legal hunt. Rickard holds a valid resident's hunting license, according to the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. Judged as a hunt, the taking of the bear complied with Fish and Game regulations, said agency spokesman Bruce Bart…

title: Clinton rejects status change for ANWR
summary: Washington -- President Clinton has rejected a proposal that he make the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge a national monument, the White House announced Wednesday. Environmentalists said they were disappointed but still hoped Clinton would change his mind before he leaves office in nine days. Proponents of exploring the refuge for oil called Clinton's decision good news but said opening ANWR won't be easy, even with a new president who is friendlier to the oil industry. An act of Congress is required to open the refuge to drilling. An area of the refuge underlying its coastal plain is considered a good prospect for a significant oil discovery. summary: Skiers and snowboarders who visit Hilltop Ski Area and Hillside Park this winter will enjoy the pleasures of a new $1.3 million ski lodge and park center, though not until the season is almost over. title: Veneer plant seeks loan
summary: Ketchikan -- The operators of a new veneer plant for this Southeast Alaska city were given the go-ahead to submit an application for a $5 million short-term loan to the Ketchikan Gateway Borough. title: Class on tracking offered
summary: EDUCATION Class on tracking offered Space remains in this winter's "Nature Observation and Tracking" class, according to the University of Alaska Anchorage. The three-credit course covers the basics of tracking and observing Alaska mammals. It includes classroom study and five, one-day field outings to find wildlife. Students taking the class should be able to function comfortably in bad weather. Classes start Monday. Cost is $467; pre-registration is required. For more, call the UAA outdoor program at 786-4066. 13-lesson boating course is set "Boating Skills and Seamanship," a 13-lesson course for recreational boaters, starts Monday at Gruening Middle School, according to Doris Thomas, spokeswoman for Flotilla 2-3 of the U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary. title: Hunting permit winners
summary: You are a winner of an Alaska sheep or moose hunting permit, maybe. At least the odds were better this time around. A significant number of hunters apparently missed the shift in deadlines from spring to winter for some permit hunts, according to the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. The state agency moved the application deadline for Koyukuk moose hunts and all Dall sheep hunts to get permits to hunters earlier, thus providing them time to prepare for late summer hunts. Instead of applying for a Chugach sheep hunting permit before Labor Day, for instance, and waiting until only a couple weeks before the sheep season to find out whether such a hunt would be possible, hunters will now know in February whether they're going to be able to participate in one of those immensely popular Chugac…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | E. COLI OUTBREAK SENDS 3 TO PENINSULA HOSPITAL By Ann Potempa Daily News Reporter (Published July 21, 2000) Three people were hospitalized and a restaurant in Sterling has voluntarily closed because of an E. coli outbreak in the Kenai Peninsula this week. The state's Division of Epidemiology said it began investigating an E. coli outbreak this week and informed physicians about four cases confirmed by laboratory studies. The victims were 26 to 70 years old and living in the area surrounding Kenai, Soldotna and Sterling. Three were treated at Central Peninsula General Hospital in Soldotna, said Marty Richman, the hospital's chief executive officer. title: McCarthy tussles over footbridge
summary: The first time the bollards were torn out of the footbridge into McCarthy, the state waited until spring to replace them. The steel-and-concrete posts had been installed to keep vehicles off the bridge. Summer was the time everyone worried about, when the tourists arrived. The second time the bollards were torn out, highway workers called the troopers. After the third time, state workers filled a 10-inch steel pipe with a steel I-beam and concrete, welded it to a 3-foot-square plate, and buried the plate five feet deep in the ground. Six weeks ago, the bollard-busters, as they are known locally, used a torch and bulldozer to clear away that latest obstruction to motorized access. This week, when the state Department of Transportation begins a series of public meetings on parking and relate…

title: Some biologists see a nightmare looming around Southeast villages after four decades of harvesting timber
summary: Editors note: Through the last two decades, a frenzy of logging occurred across Southeast Alaska as newly created Native corporations, endowed with old-growth hemlock, Sitka spruce and cedar, fired up their chain saws on behalf of their shareholders. In all, this burst of logging clear-cut an estimated 245,000 acres and shipped an estimated $2.7 billion worth of timber to mills. Daily News reporter Paula Dobbyn spent months looking into the legacy of this logging. Today we report on concerns by biologists and others that the clear-cutting will leave a lasting impact on wildlife populations and fish runs, both of which are currently healthy. Next Sunday we report on the social impact on Southeast Natives who, now that most of their trees are gone, debate whether the short-term windfall and…

title: New charges filed in strippers' case
summary: Immigration fraud charges against a Chugiak couple accused of luring Russian women to Anchorage and then forcing them to strip at a local nightclub have been boosted to kidnapping, forced labor and interstate transportation of minors for immoral purposes. A new 23-count indictment returned Wednesday names Tony and Rachel Kennard, both longtime Alaska residents; Victor Virchenko, a Russian who delivered the women to Anchorage; and Pavel Agafonov, a Russian-American who lives in Marietta, Ga. The case is the first prosecution in the United States under a new anti-exploitation law passed by Congress in October and aimed at traffickers in human beings, especially women and children, said U.S. Attorney Bob Bundy. "All across the country we're hearing more and more about people who want to come …

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Oil field spurs high expectations BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. reported Friday that it expects the new Aurora oil field on the North Slope to produce 15,000 to 20,000 barrels of oil a day. Aurora, a 30 million to 45 million barrel field next to Prudhoe Bay, began production late last year. BP, the operator of the Prudhoe field, drilled sideways into the Aurora reservoir from an existing Prudhoe production pad. The field is now producing about 7,000 barrels of oil daily. The field is expected to reach full production in the coming months, according to BP. Besides BP, the field is owned mainly by Exxon Mobil and Phillips Petroleum. Prudhoe oil spill exceeds estimates State officials say that an oil spill discovered Tuesday in Prudhoe Bay is larger than expected. Ed Meggert, spill coordinator…

title: Networks drawn to paint ball tale
summary: A videotape showing three Anchorage youths shooting paint balls at Alaska Native pedestrians is capturing the attention of national television. Anchorage Police Chief Walt Monegan Since Anchorage police released the tape Friday, the department has fielded calls from "Dateline NBC," "The Today Show," "Good Morning America," the Discovery Channel, "Inside Edition," Court TV and a Los Angeles television station. Diane Sawyer has interviewed Police Chief Walt Monegan about the incident, said police spokesman Ron McGee, and a crew from "Dateline NBC" is in town this week working on its own piece. "Unfortunately, it's the type of story that attracts this kind of attention," said Denise Burger, director of Mayor George Wuerch's office. The videotape, shot by the youths themselves, is a 24-minute …

title: Interior nominee familiar with Alaska issues
summary: President-elect George W. Bush on Friday named his secretary of the Interior: Gale Norton, a former Colorado attorney general known as a champion of the rights of states' private-property owners. Advocates of development in Alaska say they are pleased with the nomination. Environmentalists say they're are concerned. Tribal advocates say it could have been worse. Alaska Attorney General Bruce Botelho said he knows Norton as a thoughtful, intelligent moderate. "The one thing that is clear about her style is she is a consensus-builder," he said. If the Senate confirms her, she would have a key role in Bush's pledged effort to open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling. summary: A U.S. Air Force E-3 Sentry AWACS took off into a brilliant winter sun from Elmendorf Air Force Base on Friday afternoon to join the multinational task force enforcing the no-fly zone north of the 36th parallel in Iraq. Destined for duty in the Mideast, an E-3 Sentry AWACS jet lifts off Friday afternoon from Elmendorf Air Force Base. title: Sealaska Corp. faces losses
summary: It's been a rocky year for Sealaska Corp. The sizable Juneau-based Native corporation is expected to report significant losses for the first time in 17 years. Citing volatility on Wall Street and two disappointing business ventures in plastics manufacture and limestone mining, Sealaska president and chief executive Robert Loescher said the corporation navigated "rough waters" in 2000. title: Wax job will improve your performance on slopes
summary: Salt Lake City -- He never saw himself in a career obsessing about well-moisturized pores while sweating over a clothes iron. But ironing boards is what he does. Kyle Marston, a German-born ski technician at The Canyons, stands with an iron in his clean, bright shop every day up at the resort, melting wax into the bases of skis and hot- ironing it into the pores. He can put the zing back in your skis, the shred back in your snowboard. "It's no fun having a friend stay eight (lift) chairs ahead of you all day because of your wax," he said. Dry boards don't slide. Wax provides key moisture by filling in micro-pores on the sliding surface of a ski. When these pores are warm, they open and ironing fills them with wax. title: State agencies say they lack funds to enforce timber laws
summary: The law that regulates logging in Alaska requires three state agencies to oversee timber cutting on private and state land to ensure it doesn't jeopardize fish, wildlife and water quality. But in the most recent Board of Forestry report to the governor, state forester Jeff Jahnke said underfunding has enfeebled the state's ability to enforce the Forest Practices Act. "Even with federal funding, field presence is below adequate levels," wrote Jahnke. The three resource agencies received more than $650,000 in state funding last year to enforce Alaska logging standards. The federal government contributed just over $1 million. summary: It's hard to see now, all covered with snow. But in the summer, back behind a white 12-foot-wide mobile home in East Anchorage, broccoli, cauliflower and carrots grow. Ferns flourish among boulders in front. Tomatoes ripen in the greenhouse. For Clark Richelderfer and Judy Billington, it's a little Garden of Eden. "The reality is, the entire park will be shut down because it's not a productive park," Neeser said. "It's fallen into disrepair. It's a dangerous park." By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published February 18, 2001) With Fur Rendezvous sled dog races canceled this year because of snow conditions, Anchorage retailers, hoteliers, cabbies and restaurateurs are banking on the upcoming 2001 Special Olympics World Winter Games to help them recoup their losses. Salmon burgers made from pink salmon. Russian pink salmon, unfortunately. It's understandable, but regrettable, that McDonald's in Japan buys Russian pink salmon. summary: A fiercely divided U.S. Supreme Court on Wednesday struck down a Nebraska law banning "partial birth" abortions, and the ruling dooms a similar Alaska law, state Attorney General Bruce Botelho said. The 5-4 decision, written by Justice Stephen Breyer, said the Nebraska ban is too broad and does not provide an exception for cases in which the woman's health is at stake. Loren Leman, the main sponsor of the ban in the Alaska Legislature, said Breyer was wrong and that the law didn't have to contain an exception for a woman's health.
